coredata.dc:title: Enhancing visible-light driven photocatalytic performance of BiOBr by self-doping and in-situ deposition strategy: A synergistic effect between Bi5+ and metallic Bi
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Qiangshun
Chai, Siqi
Yang, Hanpei
Gao, Zhao
Zhang, Ruichen
Wang, Lina
Kang, Li
coredata.dc:description: A novel composite photocatalyst of Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ was successfully developed via a facile two-step procedure. The co-existence of self-doped Bi5+ and in-situ deposited Bi(M) was evidenced by X-ray diffraction, field-emission transmission electron microscope and X-ray photoelectron spectra. Its photocatalytic performance on the Rhodamine B (RhB) degradation under visible light irradiation displayed a significant improvement compared with that of Bi/BiOBr or BiOBr-Bi5+, which was mainly attributed to the synergistic effect between Bi(M) and Bi5+. For Bi(M), enhancement of visible-light absorption capacity was beneficial from surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect while the promoted separation of photoinduced charge carriers was attributed to the formation of oxygen vacancies (OVs) and Schottky-barrier. Besides, Bi(M) induced a predominant exposure of (010) facet, further enhancing photocatalytic performance. Differently, Bi5+ brought a narrowed bandgap of BiOBr in increasing the carrier density. A plausible mechanism towards RhB degradation was proposed based on analysis of electron transfer pathway and determination of dominant active species. The work offers new routes to enhance the photocatalytic performance of Bi-based materials without introducing any impurities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi5+ self-doping
Metallic Bi in-situ deposition
Synergistic effect
Oxygen vacancy
Schottky-barrier

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synergistic effect between Bi5+ and Bi(M)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
National Key Scientific Instrument and Equipment Development Projects of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of pristine and modified BiOBr (a) and partial magnified detail of panel a (b).
TEM (insets) and HRTEM images of pristine and modified BiOBr.
XPS spectra of survey (a), Bi 4f (b), O 1s (c) and Br 3d (d) for the pristine and modified BiOBr.
N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore diameter distribution (inset) of the pristine and modified BiOBr.

Photocatalytic degradation of RhB with different catalysts under visible light irradiation: (I) BiOBr; (II) BiOBr-Bi5+; (III) Bi/BiOBr; (IV) Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ (a) and pseudo-first-order kinetic rate constants (inset: dynamic fitting curves) (b).
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (a), Kubelka-Munk function curves (b), Photoluminescence (PL) spectra (c), and EPR spectra (d) of the pristine and modified BiOBr.
Mott-Schottky curves of BiOBr and BiOBr-Bi5+.
The band structures of BiOBr, metallic Bi and Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+.

The cyclic experiments of RhB degradation over Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+(a); the XRD pattern of fresh and recovered Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ (b); the XPS spectra of survey (c) and Bi 4f (d) of fresh and recovered Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+.

Degradation rates of RhB over Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ with different scavengers (reaction time: 30 min) (a) and ESR spectra of
O2 − (b), e− (c) and
OH (d).
A schematic diagram of RhB degradation over Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ under visible light irradiation.
BET specific surface area and corresponding pore diameter of the pristine and modified BiOBr.

head.ce:title: Enhancing visible-light driven photocatalytic performance of BiOBr by self-doping and in-situ deposition strategy: A synergistic effect between Bi5+ and metallic Bi

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Integrated Regulation and Resource Development on Shallow Lakes, Ministry of Education, Hohai University, Nanjing 210098, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Integrated Regulation and Resource Development on Shallow Lakes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Integrated Regulation and Resource Development on Shallow Lakes, Ministry of Education, Hohai University, Nanjing 210098, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment, Hohai University, Nanjing 210098, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environment, Hohai University, Nanjing 210098, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel composite photocatalyst of Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ was successfully developed via a facile two-step procedure. The co-existence of self-doped Bi5+ and in-situ deposited Bi(M) was evidenced by X-ray diffraction, field-emission transmission electron microscope and X-ray photoelectron spectra. Its photocatalytic performance on the Rhodamine B (RhB) degradation under visible light irradiation displayed a significant improvement compared with that of Bi/BiOBr or BiOBr-Bi5+, which was mainly attributed to the synergistic effect between Bi(M) and Bi5+. For Bi(M), enhancement of visible-light absorption capacity was beneficial from surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect while the promoted separation of photoinduced charge carriers was attributed to the formation of oxygen vacancies (OVs) and Schottky-barrier. Besides, Bi(M) induced a predominant exposure of (010) facet, further enhancing photocatalytic performance. Differently, Bi5+ brought a narrowed bandgap of BiOBr in increasing the carrier density. A plausible mechanism towards RhB degradation was proposed based on analysis of electron transfer pathway and determination of dominant active species. The work offers new routes to enhance the photocatalytic performance of Bi-based materials without introducing any impurities.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decades, photocatalytic oxidation using nano-semiconductors as catalysts, as one of efficacious and sustainable technology, has attracted tremendous attention due to their potential applications in the field of environmental remediation, including CO2 transformation into valuable fuel [1,2], heavy metal ions reduction into less toxic ones [3], and organic pollutant decomposition and mineralization [4]. To date, metallic oxides or sulfides like TiO2 [5], ZnO [6], ZnS [7] and etc. have been served as photocatalysts to tackle with environmental contaminates. Nevertheless, these UV-driven photocatalysts suffer from their inherent drawback of narrow range in light-absorption owing to their large bandgap values [8]. It is highly desired to develop visible-light driven photocatalysts which can utilize the high portion (ca. 43%) of visible light in the overall solar spectrum [9]. Considering the advantages as suitable band structure, earth abundance, cost effectiveness, stability and environmental friendliness, Bismuth based (Bi-based) materials have drawn intensive research interests [10–13], especially the promising bismuth oxybromide (BiOBr) possessing an unique layer structure characterized by tetragonal slabs of positively charged [Bi2O2] interleaved by double slabs of negatively charged Br atoms to form [Br-Bi-O-Bi-Br] layers along the c-axis [14]. An internal electronic field (IEF) can self-generate between [Bi2O2] and Br slabs to effectively manipulate the transport of photoinduced carriers, and simultaneously, a hybridization between Bi 6s and O 2p in BiOBr can narrow the bandgap of BiOBr and endow it the capacity of visible-light absorption [15], both of which are favorable for its photocatalytic performance. However, the actual performance of BiOBr towards pollutant degradation under visible light irradiation is still far from satisfactory.

Till date, many efforts and attempts have been endeavored including construction of semiconductor heterojunctions [16,17], impurity doping [18] and surface metallization [19] to improve the photocatalytic performance of BiOBr. Amongst them, impurity doping and surface metallization have been extensively employed for their simplicity and effectiveness in extending the spectral response to visible light region by bandgap-narrowing [20] or surface plasma resonance (SPR) effect [21], and in preventing charge carrier recombination by Schottky barrier induced at the metal–semiconductor interface [21]. For instance, Yu et al. [22] prepared a series of noble metal (Rh, Pd, Pt)/BiOX(Cl, O, Br) composites and proved that noble metal deposition enhanced photocatalytic performance of BiOX due to a promoted separation rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Liu et al. [23] synthesized a Pt/I-BiOBr and found that doped I− played a main effect on broadening light absorption region while loaded Pt mainly promote the separation of photoinduced charge carriers for bisphenol A degradation. However, the thermo-instability, poor crystallinity and cost ineffectiveness caused by heteroatom doping and noble metal deposition impinge on the utilization of these strategies [24,25].

Alternatively, more and more attention has been paid to modification of BiOBr by self-doping of Bi5+ or in-situ deposition of metallic Bi (Bi(M)) to avoid introduction of any undesirable impurities. Wu et al. [26] prepared Bi5+ self-doped BiOBr under UV irradiation and comparatively superior photocatalytic activity was shown for phenol decomposition and E. coli inactivation, which was assigned to the narrowed bandgap, enhanced visible light absorption capacity and electron trapping effect of Bi5+. Noteworthily, metallic Bi, a typical semimetal, have been widely evaluated as a substitute to noble metals due to its cheapness, non-toxicity, Fermi surface with high anisotropy, long free path of carriers, and small band gap [27]. Gao et al. [28] synthesized Bi/BiOBr with a facile hydrothermal method for RhB degradation and the improved photocatalytic properties was attributed to its high BET specific area, narrow energy band gap, and inhibited carrier recombination. Inspired by the respective advantages of Bi5+ and Bi(M), we try to dope Bi5+ in the crystal lattice and deposit Bi(M) on the surface of BiOBr simultaneously and explore their joint effect on the photocatalytic performance of pristine BiOBr. To the best of our knowledge, the research in this aspect has not been reported in literatures.

Herein, for the first time, the novel composite photocatalyst of Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ was synthesized via the facile two-step approach in which Bi(M) was in-situ deposited by glucose reduction in the hydrothermal process and then Bi5+ was yielded under UV irradiation. Structure and physicochemical properties of as-prepared samples were characterized and their photocatalytic performance was evaluated by degradation of Rhodamine B under visible light. As expected, Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ exhibited exceptionally high efficiency for RhB removal compared with its counterparts (BiOBr, Bi/BiOBr and BiOBr-Bi5+), which was mainly ascribed to the synergistic effect between Bi5+ and Bi(M).

All chemicals and reagents were of analytic grade and used as received. Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3⋅5H2O), potassium bromide (KBr), and glucose were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Nitric acid (HNO3) was obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). De-ionized (DI) water used in experiments was produced from an ultra-pure water equipment (UPT-11-10T, ULUPURE, China).

BiOBr with Bi(M) in-situ deposition (Bi/BiOBr) was fabricated by a hydrothermal method detailed as follows: firstly, 4 mmol Bi(NO3)3⋅5H2O was homogeneously dispersed in 15 mL aqueous solution of 0.3 M HNO3 and meanwhile, 4 mmol KBr was dissolved in 50 mL DI water; then, the two solutions were mixed by vigorous stirring. After that, 1 g glucose was added into the mixed liquid and then stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, the resultant solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon autoclave and heated at 170 °C for 8 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature (∼25 °C), the solids were collected by a high-speed centrifuge (8000 rpm) followed by their washing with DI water repeatedly and drying at 60 °C for 4 h. The preparation procedure for BiOBr was similar as above but with no addition of glucose.

The Bi5+ self-doped photocatalysts (BiOBr-Bi5+ and Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+) were synthesized according to the reported method with assistance of UV irradiation [26]. Specifically, a suspension containing 100 mg freshly synthesized BiOBr or Bi/BiOBr and 100 mL DI water was exposed to UV light (200 ≤ λ < 420 nm) for 0.5 h under continuous stirring. The UV light was originated from 300 W xenon lamp (CEL-HXUV-300, CEAULIGHT, Beijing, China) equipped with a UV reflector. After irradiation, the products were collected via centrifugation and then washed with DI water for three times and finally dried at 60 °C for 4 h.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis were performed using a Shimadzu-3A diffractometer at 40 kV and 30 mA with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm). The morphologies were observed by field emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained by a PHI 5000 Versa Probe spectrometer (ULVAC-PHI, JEOL) operated at 13 kV and 28 mA using Al Kα as an exciting source (1486.6 eV). The binding energies were corrected based on C 1s at 284.6 eV, and the data analysis was performed using the XPSPEAK4.1 program with a mixed Lorentzian-Gaussian function for curve fitting and background subtraction via a Shirley function. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were obtained with a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer equipped with an integrated sphere using BaSO4 as reference. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer with an emission wavelength of 325 nm. The specific surface area and pore diameter distribution of samples was measured by N2 adsorption–desorption on Micromeritics ASAP 2020 based on the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) and Barret-Joyner-Halender (BJH) method, respectively. The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of radical spin-trapped by 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was acquired on a JES FA200 spectrometer, and the corresponding reactive oxygen species (ROS) were determined in methanolic (DMPO-
O2 −) and aqueous dispersions (DMPO-
OH), respectively. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were obtained on a Bruker ESP 500 spectrometer at room temperature.

The electrochemical measurements were carried out on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation with a standard three-electrode system in aqueous solution of Na2SO4 (0.2 M). The working electrode was prepared as follows: first, each sample and trace nafion were ultrasonically dispersed in ethanol, and then the obtained liquid was spread on a tin fluoride conductor glass within 1 × 1 cm area. The platinum wire and Ag/AgCl were functioned as the counter and reference electrode, respectively. Mott–Schottky plots of samples were conducted in the same system of standard three-electrodes at a frequency of 1 kHz.

In order to evaluate the visible-light driven photocatalytic activity of as-prepared samples, Rhodamine B (RhB), as a typical cationic dye, was chosen as model pollutant. For each run, 25 mg of photocatalyst was added into 100 mL solution of RhB (10 mg/L) and then the obtained suspension was magnetically stirred in dark at 500 rpm for 30 min to reach adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Subsequently, a 300 W xenon lamp (CEL-HXUV-300, CEAULIGHT, Beijing, China) equipped with an ultraviolet cutoff filter (λ > 420 nm), as visible light source, was turned on to trigger the photocatalytic reaction. During the photodegradation process, cold water was circulated to maintain the temperature of system. At the given interval of 10 min, 3 mL aliquot was sampled and filtered through 0.45 μm polyethersulfone syringe filters to measure the absorbance of residual RhB by a dual-beam UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU-1900, PERSEE, China) at maximum absorption wavelength (λmax) of 554 nm. Quenching experiments were also conducted in a similar way with the addition of each scavenger, i.e., disodiumethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA-2Na, for h+), tert-Butyl alcohol (TBA, for
OH) and ascorbic acid (for
O2 −), respectively.

XRD patterns of as-prepared pristine and modified BiOBr are shown in Fig. 1 (a). All the sharp diffraction peaks of BiOBr can be well indexed to the tetragonal phase of BiOBr (PDF #078-0348) [29], and no peaks of other phase were detected, suggesting the high crystallization and purity of BiOBr. Of note, the characteristic peaks of Bi5+ self-doped BiOBr (BiOBr-Bi5+ and Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+) were similar with that of BiOBr but the peaks of (012) and (110) crystal planes slightly shifted to higher diffraction angles (as seen in Fig. 1(b)), indicating the decreased lattice spacing in view of Bragg’s law (nλ = 2dsin2θ). Thus, it can be inferred that Bi5+ could be self-doped into the lattice of BiOBr by UV irradiation. Additionally, it can be seen that after in-situ deposition of Bi(M), the peak intensity of (001) was significantly decreased whereas that of (012) increased, illustrating that the preferential orientation of crystal growth changed from (001) to (012) plane, which may result in the variation of predominant exposed facet. Furthermore, the characteristic diffraction peaks ascribed to monoclinic Bi (PDF#65-1215) [30] occurred in the XRD pattern of Bi/BiOBr and Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+, evidencing that partial Bi3+ in precursors was successfully transformed into Bi(M) via glucose reduction during the hydrothermal process. Nevertheless, the peak intensities of Bi(M) are extremely low, which may be due to low content or high dispersion of Bi(M) in the composites [31].

TEM and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) were employed to observe their morphologies and the predominate exposed crystal planes. As displayed in TEM images (inset of Fig. 2 ), all the samples exhibit similar architecture of square-like nanosheets indicating that Bi5+ or Bi(M) modification basically has no influence on their morphologies as consistent with the reported literature [26]. Their sizes, however, are distinctly different, 0.5–1.5 μm for BiOBr and BiOBr-Bi5+ but 0.2–0.5 μm for Bi/BiOBr and Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+. The significant decrease in size is linked to the generation of Bi(M) that consumes a part of the Bi3+ precursor [32]. Besides, scattered round particles with diameter of ca. 100 nm are clearly observed on the surface of BiOBr, further corroborating that Bi(M) was successfully deposited on the surface of BiOBr in the hydrothermal reduction process.

HRTEM images (viewed from [001] axis) of BiOBr and BiOBr-Bi5+ clearly show two sets of perpendicular lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.277 nm corresponding to that of (110) and (1
1 ¯
0) facets [33,34], manifesting that BiOBr predominately grow along the [110] direction. Consequently, the highly exposed (001) plane was well-preserved for Bi5+-doped samples. However, as for Bi/BiOBr and Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+, lattice fringes with d spacings of about 0.282 nm (as shown in Fig. 2(c)) are attributed to (102) plane, which are vertical to the (010) plane. Thus, it can be concluded that predominant exposed facet was changed from (001) to (010) after Bi(M) deposition (see Fig. 3 ). Li et al. reported that BiOBr with highly exposed (010) facet exhibited higher photocatalytic activity than those with dominant exposed (001) facet [33]. Thus, Bi(M) in-situ deposition contributes to the photocatalytic activity of BiOBr via structural adjustment. Moreover, lattice fringes with interplanar spacing of 0.313 nm ascribed to (200) plane of Bi(M) were also observed, evidencing the existence of Bi(M), which is in a good agreement with the results from XRD.

XPS spectra were measured to determine the surficial element composition and chemical states. The survey spectra in Fig. 4 (a) reveal that apart from adventitious carbon, three main elements, viz., Bi, O and Br coexist in the as-prepared samples. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the Bi 4f spectrum of BiOBr shows typical peaks from Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 of Bi3+ at 164.66 and 159.36 eV, respectively [35]. In comparison, new peaks ascribed to Bi(M) [36] in Bi/BiOBr were observed at 162.60 and 157.21 eV, while peaks positioned at 165.15 and 159.85 eV can be extracted and attributed to the higher valence state of Bi as Bi5+ in BiOBr-Bi5+ [26]. In the Bi 4f spectrum of Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+, all peaks from Bi(M), Bi3+, and Bi5+ are exhibited, suggesting that Bi(M) in-situ deposition and Bi5+ self-doping were successfully achieved during the two-step synthesis process.

In the O 1s spectrum of BiOBr (Fig. 4(c)), peaks at 531.20 and 530.16 eV are assigned to the surface (OH and chemically adsorbed O) and lattice oxygen (Bi2O2 2+) in the sample, respectively [37]. Obviously, the amount of surface oxygen in modified BiOBr (Bi/BiOBr, BiOBr-Bi5+ and Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+) is much more than that of pristine BiOBr, which may result from the presence of oxygen vacancies (OVs). The Br 3d spectra (Fig. 4(d)) can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 69.46 and 68.42 eV, which can be assigned to Br 3d3/2 and 3d5/2, respectively [38]. It is worth noting that the binding energies of Bi 4f, O 1s and Br 3d in all Bi(M)-deposited samples are lower than those of the pristine one. As reported, the increase in surficial electron density could decrease the binding energy [39]. Therefore, Bi(M) in-situ deposition could increase the surficial electron density of BiOBr, which could be reasonably explained by the change of exposed facet from (001) to (010) detected by HRTEM. In addition, the content of Bi(M) on Bi/BiOBr and Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ is calculated to be 5.06% and 4.32%, respectively, the values of which are very similar. Thus, it can be deduced that Bi5+ in Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ was mainly derived from the oxidation of Bi3+ rather than Bi(M).

The N2 adsorption–desorption was performed to analyze the influence of self-doping of Bi5+ and in-situ deposition of Bi(M) on the specific surface area and pore diameter of BiOBr. As shown in Fig. 5 , all samples exhibit representative isotherms of type Ⅳ with hysteresis loops between 0.8 and 1.0 relative pressure (P/P 0), implying the existence of mesopores according to IUPAC classification, which might be formed by the aggregation of BiOBr nanosheets. Notably, with deposition of Bi(M), macropores were also generated during the hydrothermal process (inset of Fig. 5). The specific surface area and pore diameter calculated based on the method of BET and BJH are summarized in Table 1 and it can be found that Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ has the largest specific surface area (25.41 cm2/g), higher than that of Bi/BiOBr (22.13 cm2/g) and BiOBr-Bi5+ (14.07 cm2/g). In addition, the average pore diameter (2.15 nm) of Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ is also largest among them due to the generation of more mesopores and macropores, which is conducive to mass transfer of reactants and subsequent photocatalytic degradation reaction [40].

The photocatalytic activity of pure and modified BiOBr was evaluated via degradation of RhB under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), prior to light irradiation, a small part of RhB molecules were removed via adsorption of photocatalysts and their adsorption capacity is in the sequence of Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ > Bi/BiOBr > BiOBr > BiOBr-Bi5+, which is consistent with their sequence in specific surface area. Blank experiment (without catalyst addition) shows negligible degradation of RhB, indicating that RhB is stable under visible light illumination. BiOBr brought ca. 40.7% removal of RhB, and BiOBr-Bi5+ exhibited a little bit increase of its removal efficiency to 45.6% whereas that of Bi/BiOBr (97.6%) increased remarkably, implying that Bi(M) plays a leading role in improving photocatalytic performance. Notably, in the case of Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+, complete removal of RhB was achieved within 40 min, implying that Bi(M) and Bi5+ co-modification is obviously superior to Bi(M) in-situ deposition or Bi5+ self-doping for the activity. For further quantitative comparison, the photodegradation data were fitted to pseudo-first-order kinetics and it can be found from Fig. 6(b) that the reaction rate constant (0.0932 min−1) of Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ is much higher than the sum (0.0647 min−1) of that of Bi/BiOBr and BiOBr-Bi5+, indicating that there is a synergistic effect between Bi(M) and Bi5+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergistic effect between Bi5+ and Bi(M)
ce:section.ce:para:
To elucidate the synergistic effect between Bi5+ and Bi(M), the optical properties of pure and modified BiOBr were first studied by UV–vis DRS. As displayed in Fig. 7 (a), pure BiOBr has strong absorption in the UV region but very weak one in the visible light region with an absorption edge around 425 nm. Noteworthily, with Bi5+ doping, its absorption in UV region decreased significantly whereas that in visible light region increased in a widened spectral range. As for Bi/BiOBr, increase of absorption in both region of UV and visible light was observed due to the strong surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of Bi(M) [32,41]. The band gap of pure and Bi5+-doped BiOBr were estimated based on Kubelka-Munk equation (Eq. (1)) [4] as:
(1)
ahv =
A ( h v -
E g
)
n / 2
where α, hv, A and E g represent the absorption coefficient, photonic energy, constant, and bandgap value, respectively. As all the pure and modified BiOBr are indirect semiconductors, n is 4 in Eq. (1). As shown in Fig. 7(b), the E g of BiOBr and BiOBr-Bi5+ were calculated to be 2.84 and 2.61 eV, respectively. Thus, the bandgap of BiOBr was narrowed by Bi5+ self-doping. Nevertheless, it is found from Fig. 6(a) that the removal efficiency of BiOBr-Bi5+ for RhB merely increased a little bit compared with that of Bi/BiOBr, which is presumably resulted from its fast recombination of photoinduced carriers. On the contrary, the high efficiency obtained for Bi(M) deposited samples can be attributed to their high separation efficiency of charge carriers.

In order to demonstrate the above hypothesis, photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were thereafter measured under excitation at λ of 325 nm. Generally, the lower PL intensity means the higher separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes. The steady-state PL spectra (Fig. 7(c)) show that the broad PL emission recorded on BiOBr in 400–500 nm are strongest, indicating the highest recombination rate of photoinduced charge carriers [42]. It is worth noting that the intensity of PL emission weakened slightly on BiOBr-Bi5+ while much on Bi/BiOBr and the most on Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+, and this strong-to-weak sequence accords well with that in photocatalytic activities. Owing to larger work function of Bi(M), the formation of Schottky barrier [34] is beneficial for transfer of photo-generated electrons, and thus most of photo-generated electrons was transferred from BiOBr to Bi(M). As for BiOBr-Bi5+, however, only a small number of photo-generated electrons were captured by Bi5+ leading to unobvious improvement of its photocatalytic performance. Thus, Bi(M) plays a leading role in separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

It is well acknowledged that the introduction of dopants into matrix always results in the generation of crystal defects [43,44], which can be probed by the normal temperature solid-state electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) technique. As shown in Fig. 7(d), the four samples show signal peaks with different intensities at a g ≈ 2.004 that is considered to be the characteristic of surface oxygen vacancies (OVs) [45]. Compared with pristine BiOBr, a much stronger EPR signal was detected for Bi/BiOBr suggesting that Bi(M) deposition is beneficial to the generation of OVs, which coincides with the result of XPS spectra of O 1 s. Due to gradual reduction of Bi3+ into Bi(M) in the hydrothermal process, the generation of OVs can be well explained by the charge compensation [46]. In general, OVs, as electron sinks, tend to trap the excited electrons and facilitate the migration of holes [47]. Consequently, for Bi(M) deposited samples, electron transfer occurred through two pathways, viz., Schottky barrier and OVs. Combined with the results of UV–vis, PL and ESR, it can be concluded that the synergist effects are mainly derived from two aspects: on the one hand, Bi(M) deposition can induce the generation of oxygen vacancies (OVs) and Schottky barrier leading to efficient separation of photo-induced charge carriers, and besides, the intensified visible light absorption capacity resulted from SPR effect of Bi(M); on the other hand, Bi5+ self-doping can narrow the bandgap of pure BiOBr resulting in increase of carrier density, which will be authenticated later.

Given that band gap of pure BiOBr was narrowed after Bi5+ self-doping, the band edge positions of valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) were further investigated with Mott-Schottky measurements [48]. The flat band potential and carrier density of samples can be evaluated based on the following equation:
(2)
1
C
SC
2
=
2
ε
ε 0
A
2
e
N d
E -
E
fb
-
k B
T
e
where ε refers to dielectric constant, ε0 is the vacuum dielectric constant, A corresponds to the area of electrode, e is the electron charge, N d is the donor density, E is the applied voltage, E fb is the flat band potential, kB is the Bohr Zimmer constant, and T is the absolute temperature. Herein, kBT/e is about 25 mV at room temperature, which can be ignored. Obviously, both BiOBr and BiOBr-Bi5+ are identified as n-type semiconductors owing to the positive slope of Mott-Schottky plots. As displayed in Fig. 8 , the flat band potentials of BiOBr and BiOBr-Bi5+ can be confirmed to be −0.57 V (-0.37 V vs.NHE) and −0.48 V (-0.28 V vs. NHE), respectively, which can be considered as the position of Fermi level. According to the reported literatures, the minimum conduction band (CB) is higher about 0.1 eV than the flat-band for n-type semiconductor. Therefore, the E CB of BiOBr and Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ could be estimated to be −0.47 V and −0.38 V (vs. NHE), respectively. Their corresponding E VB can be calculated according to the formula as follows:
(3)
E
VB
=
E
CB
+
E g

Thus, the E VB of BiOBr and BiOBr-Bi5+ are determined to be 2.37 and 2.23 V. The approximate energy band diagrams of samples are illustrated in Fig. 9 and it can be found that Schottky barrier was formed at the interface between Bi(M) and BiOBr-Bi5+, which can be regarded as a transfer channel for photoinduced electrons. Based on this channel, charged carriers can be effectively separated. Moreover, the carrier density of BiOBr-Bi5+ was confirmed to be 6.56 × 1021 cm−3, 1.57 times that of BiOBr (4.16 × 1021cm−3). It has been well established that high carrier density is conducive to enhancement of energy conversion efficiency [49]. Thus, compared with pure BiOBr, more photogenerated electrons are available for BiOBr-Bi5+ under visible light irradiation, beneficial for following photocatalytic degradation reaction.

As is well known, the reusability and stability of photocatalysts are of great importance for the practical application. Thus, the cyclic degradation experiments were carried out with Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ as photocatalyst for six times. As shown in Fig. 10 (a), no obvious change of RhB degradation curves was observed up to 6th cycle, indicating that the photocatalyst of Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ have excellent reusability. In addition, the crystal structure, surficial element compositions and chemical states before and after six cycles were also examined, respectively. It can be found from Fig. 10(b) that the crystal structure of recovered samples kept intact compared with that of fresh ones. Also, the surface element compositions and the valence states of Bi remained unchanged after six repetition experiments (Fig. 10(c) and (d)). Therefore, the as-prepared Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ have outstanding reusability and stability, showing great promise for practical application.

To determine the main active species produced during the photocatalytic reaction, a series of radical trapping experiments were carried out. T-butyl alcohol (TBA), ascorbic acid and disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA-2Na) were served as the quenchers of hydroxyl radical (
OH), superoxide radical (
O2 −), and hole (h+), respectively [50]. As can be seen in Fig. 11 (a), RhB decomposition was significantly inhibited with addition of ascorbic acid or EDTA-2Na while merely slight decrease was observed for TBA as scavengers, suggesting that
O2 − and h+ were dominant active species responsible for pollutant degradation. In order to further confirm above result, ESR measurements were also conducted. As shown in Fig. 11(b) and (c), gradually increased signal peaks attributed to DMPO-
O2 − and decreased ones ascribed to electron (e−) were observed with elongation of illumination time, indicating that the
O2 − was generated through consuming photoinduced e−. Nevertheless, no signal peaks belonged to DMPO-
OH were detected (Fig. 11(d)), which is in accordance with the results of the trapping experiments.

On the basis of the above analysis, a plausible mechanism for RhB degradation over Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ under visible light irradiation was tentatively proposed and the schematic diagram is presented as shown in Fig. 12 . First, high-valence Bi (Bi5+) self-doping can narrow the bandgap of pristine BiOBr, thus broadening the photoabsorption range and enhancing the carrier density. However, the photocatalytic performance of BiOBr-Bi5+ is largely limited by high recombination of photo-generated electron and hole. Interestingly, with in-situ deposition of Bi(M), the photo-induced charge carriers can be efficiently separated owing to the formation of OVs and Schottky barrier. On the one hand, OVs, as electron traps, can effectively capture photogenerated electrons that are excited from valence band of BiOBr-Bi5+; on the other hand, Schottky barrier is beneficial to electron transfer from conduction band to Bi(M). As reported by Chen et al. [46], Bi(M) could be functioned as the active site to activate molecule oxygen (O2). Thus, more available electrons located in Bi(M) tend to react with surface-adsorbed O2, further promoting the activation of O2 and improving the yield of
O2 − [51]. However, due to the less positive potential of valence band position, there is no possibility to generate
OH by direct h+ oxidation in this system. Thus, h+ and
O2 − are regarded as the main active species responsible for RhB degradation. Beneficial from SPR effect of Bi(M), more hot electrons are involved in the process of radical formation, improving the oxidation capacity of overall system. Moreover, Bi(M) deposition induced the change of predominate exposed facet of BiOBr from (001) to (010), further enhancing the photocatalytic activity under visible light. In a word, the improved photocatalytic activity of BiOBr mainly results from the synergistic effect between Bi5+ and Bi(M).

The novel photocatalyst of Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ were successfully fabricated via a facile two-step approach and characterized by a series of technologies. The characterization results indicated that in contrast with pristine BiOBr, Bi5+-doped samples basically remained the original crystal structure, appearance, size and the preferential orientation of crystal growth whereas Bi(M)-deposited samples decreased the size and changed predominant exposed facet from (001) to (010). The XPS spectra evidenced the co-existence of Bi5+ and Bi(M) in Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+. The specific surface area of photocatalysts with co-modification of Bi5+ and Bi(M) was increased remarkably. The photodegradation experiments suggested that the reaction rate constant (0.0923 min−1) of RhB over Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ was higher than the sum of that over BiOBr-Bi5+ (0.0077 min−1) and Bi/BiOBr (0.0570 min−1), showing the synergistic effect between Bi5+ and Bi(M), which was testified by UV–vis, PL and ESR. Specifically, for one thing, Bi(M) deposition can induce the generation of OVs and Schottky barrier leading to efficient separation of photoinduced charge carriers and rapid activation of adsorbed oxygen molecules; for another, intensified visible-light absorption capacity was obtained owing to SPR effect. Besides, the change of predominant exposed facet from (001) to (010) also paly partial roles in improving photocatalytic performance. As for Bi5+ self-doping, the bandgap of BiOBr could be narrowed leading to increasement of the carrier density. Bi/BiOBr-Bi5+ displayed the excellent reusability and stability, and h+ and
O2 − were determined as the main active species responsible for pollutants degradation. This work provides innovative ways to improving the photocatalytic performance of Bi-based materials without introduction of any impurities.

Qiangshun Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Siqi Chai: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Hanpei Yang: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Zhao Gao: Writing - review & editing. Ruichen Zhang: Writing - review & editing. Lina Wang: Writing - review & editing. Li Kang: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
